questions.question: Explain the difference between error detection and error correction. Which methods are used for each?
keyPoints: Error detection identifies if errors occurred; error correction not only detects but also identifies exact bit positions and corrects them
Error detection methods: Parity check, Checksum, CRC
Error correction methods: Hamming code, Forward Error Correction (FEC)
Error detection is simpler and requires fewer redundant bits than error correction

questions.question: Describe the Stop-and-Wait ARQ protocol with a neat diagram showing the sequence of frame and acknowledgment exchange.
keyPoints: Sender transmits one frame and waits for ACK
Receiver sends ACK only after receiving and processing the frame
If ACK not received within timeout, sender retransmits the same frame
If corrupted frame received, receiver discards it and doesn't send ACK
The protocol ensures reliability but has low efficiency

questions.question: Calculate the Hamming code for the data word 1101. Show the placement of data bits and parity bits.
keyPoints: Data bits: 1,1,0,1 at positions 3,5,6,7
Parity bits at positions 1,2,4 (powers of 2)
P1 (position 1) covers positions 1,3,5,7 = 1,1,1 → odd → P1=1
P2 (position 2) covers positions 2,3,6,7 = 1,0,1 → even → P2=0
P4 (position 4) covers positions 4,5,6,7 = 0,1,0,1 → even → P4=0
Final codeword: 1110101

questions.question: Explain the Sliding Window protocol and mention its advantages over Stop-and-Wait.
keyPoints: Sliding Window allows multiple frames to be in transit before acknowledgment
Sender maintains a window of sequence numbers
Window slides forward as ACKs are received
Advantages: Higher channel utilization, better efficiency, supports full-duplex communication
Disadvantages: More complex implementation, requires buffer management

questions.question: What is CRC? Explain how CRC is generated and checked at the receiver with an example.
keyPoints: CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) uses polynomial division
Sender: Divide data by generator polynomial, append remainder as CRC bits
Receiver: Divide received data by same polynomial, remainder should be zero for no error
Example: Data: 110101, Generator: 1101 (degree 3)
CRC detects burst errors up to length equal to degree of generator polynomial

questions.question: Differentiate between Go-Back-N ARQ and Selective Repeat ARQ protocols.
keyPoints: Go-Back-N: Retransmits lost frame and ALL subsequent frames
Selective Repeat: Retransmits ONLY the lost frame
Go-Back-N uses cumulative ACKs; Selective Repeat uses individual ACKs
Selective Repeat requires more buffer space at receiver for out-of-order frames
Go-Back-N is simpler but less efficient than Selective Repeat

questions.question: Explain the concept of burst error and mention techniques to handle burst errors.
keyPoints: Burst error corrupts multiple consecutive bits (2 or more)
Burst length measured from first to last corrupted bit including both
Causes: impulse noise, fading in wireless channels, hardware faults
Handling techniques: Interleaving (spread bits across time), FEC codes designed for bursts, Reed-Solomon codes
Burst errors are more common than single-bit errors in real channels

questions.question: Explain the Hamming code error correction mechanism. How many redundant bits are required for a data word of 8 bits? Determine the positions of data and parity bits and verify error correction for a single-bit error.
keyPoints: For 8 data bits: need r bits where 2^r >= 8+r+1 → 2^4=16 >= 13 → r=4
Total codeword length = 12 bits
Parity bits at positions 1,2,4,8 (powers of 2)
Data bits at positions 3,5,6,7,9,10,11,12
Explain parity check equation formation
For error detection: calculate syndrome bits
If syndrome is non-zero, error position is given by binary value of syndrome bits
Correct error by flipping the bit at error position

questions.question: Derive the efficiency of Stop-and-Wait ARQ and Sliding Window protocol. In a system with propagation delay of 10ms and transmission time of 1ms, calculate the efficiency for both protocols (assuming window size of 7 for sliding window).
keyPoints: Stop-and-Wait efficiency = Tt / (Tt + 2Tp) = 1 / (1 + 20) = 1/21 ≈ 4.76%
Sliding Window efficiency = (N × Tt) / (Tt + 2Tp) where N is window size
For window size 7: efficiency = (7 × 1) / (1 + 20) = 7/21 = 33.33%
Explain how sliding window improves efficiency
If sliding window with infinite sequence numbers: efficiency approaches 1
Formula derivation shows propagation delay dominates when Tt << 2Tp

questions.question: Describe the different types of errors in data communication. Explain any three error detection techniques with examples and compare their effectiveness.
keyPoints: Types: Single-bit errors, Burst errors
Parity checking: Add one bit to make count even/odd; detects odd errors only; simple but weak
Checksum: Use ones-complement addition of segments; better than parity; detects most random errors
CRC: Polynomial division; most powerful; detects all burst errors up to degree of generator polynomial
Comparison: CRC > Checksum > Parity in error detection capability
CRC has very low probability of undetected errors (1 in 10^9 or better)

questions.question: Explain the working of Selective Repeat ARQ with a suitable sequence diagram. What are the advantages and limitations of this protocol compared to Go-Back-N ARQ?
keyPoints: Sender maintains N separate timers for each unacknowledged frame
Receiver individually acknowledges correctly received frames
Receiver buffers out-of-order frames until missing frames arrive
When sender's timer expires for a frame, only that frame is retransmitted
Advantages over Go-Back-N: Higher throughput, less retransmission overhead, better for high-error channels
Limitations: Complex receiver logic, larger buffer requirements, potential for duplicate frames if ACK lost
Window size must be <= 2^(k-1) to avoid ambiguity between old and new frames
